ComplianceMarch 9, 20268 min read

How Long Must You Keep Crane Inspection Records? Retention Guide

You completed the inspections. You documented everything properly. Now how long do you have to keep those records? The answer depends on the record type, the standard, and your state — and getting it wrong can be as bad as never inspecting at all.

Most crane companies focus on performing inspections — which is right. But surprisingly few think about how long those records need to be retained. Then an OSHA investigator shows up asking for 3-year-old annual inspection reports, or a plaintiff's attorney subpoenas maintenance records from 5 years ago, and the company discovers their paper binders from 2021 went into a dumpster during an office move.

Record retention isn't glamorous. But destroying records too early — or not being able to find them — can turn a defensible position into a catastrophic one.

OSHA Requirements by Record Type

Frequent (Daily/Pre-Shift) Inspections

OSHA does not explicitly require documentation of frequent inspections under 1926.1412(a). However, if you do document them (and you should), there's no mandated retention period. Recommendation: Keep for a minimum of 1 year. Daily records are your first line of defense in any incident investigation.

Periodic (Monthly) Inspections

Under 1926.1412(b), periodic inspections must be documented. OSHA requires these records be maintained until the next periodic inspection of that type is completed. Recommendation: Keep for a minimum of 3 years. OSHA investigations can look back multiple years, and "we only keep the most recent one" leaves gaps in your history.

Annual Comprehensive Inspections

Under 1926.1412(c), annual inspection records must be documented and retained. OSHA requires retention until the next annual inspection is completed. Recommendation: Keep for the life of the equipment. Annual inspections establish the crane's safety trajectory over time. A complete history of annuals is your strongest audit defense.

Maintenance and Repair Records

While 1926.1417 requires maintenance procedures to be followed and documented, it doesn't specify a retention period. Recommendation: Keep for the life of the equipment plus 5 years after disposal. Maintenance history is critical for establishing that the crane was properly maintained throughout its service life.

Modification Records

Any modifications to a crane — capacity changes, structural alterations, component substitutions — must be documented permanently. Under 1926.1434, modifications require either manufacturer approval or a registered professional engineer's analysis. These records must be kept permanently because they fundamentally change the crane's operating parameters.

ANSI B30 Retention Guidelines

The ANSI B30 standards provide additional guidance beyond OSHA minimums:

  • B30.2 (Overhead and Gantry Cranes): Inspection records should be maintained for a minimum of 12 months or until the next inspection of the same type
  • B30.5 (Mobile and Locomotive Cranes): Similar retention requirements with emphasis on maintaining records of critical component inspections
  • B30.3 (Tower Cranes): Records of erection, climbing, and dismantling must be maintained alongside inspection records for the project duration plus any applicable retention requirements
  • All B30 standards: Records of load tests must be permanently maintained

State-Specific Requirements

Several states impose retention requirements that exceed OSHA's:

  • California (Cal/OSHA): Crane inspection records must be kept for at least 3 years — longer than federal OSHA's "until next inspection" minimum
  • New York City: The NYC Department of Buildings requires crane inspection and maintenance records be maintained for the life of the equipment and available for DOB review at any time
  • Washington State: L&I requires crane inspection records for a minimum of 3 years
  • Other states: Many states with their own OSHA plans (state plans) have adopted retention requirements ranging from 1-5 years. Check your state's specific requirements.

Rule of thumb: If you operate in multiple states, apply the strictest requirement across your entire fleet. It's simpler than managing different retention schedules by location.

The Records You Must Keep

Here's a complete list of crane-related records and recommended retention periods:

  • Daily pre-shift inspections: 1 year minimum, 3 years recommended
  • Monthly/periodic inspections: 3 years minimum
  • Annual comprehensive inspections: Life of equipment
  • Maintenance logs: Life of equipment + 5 years
  • Repair records: Life of equipment + 5 years
  • Modification/alteration records: Permanent
  • Load test records: Permanent
  • Wire rope inspection/replacement records: 3 years minimum
  • Operator certification records: Duration of employment + 3 years
  • Competent/qualified person designations: Duration of designation + 3 years
  • Training records: Duration of employment + 3 years
  • Accident/incident reports: 5 years minimum (OSHA 300 log requirement)
  • Manufacturer manuals and service bulletins: Life of equipment

Litigation Hold: When You Can't Destroy Anything

If your company is involved in — or reasonably anticipates — litigation, arbitration, or a government investigation, you must immediately implement a litigation hold. This means:

  • No records can be destroyed — regardless of your normal retention schedule
  • All potentially relevant records must be preserved — inspection records, maintenance logs, communications, photos, everything
  • Employees must be notified not to destroy or alter any records
  • The hold supersedes your normal retention/destruction policies

Destroying records after a litigation hold should have been implemented is called spoliation of evidence. Courts can impose sanctions, adverse inference instructions (the jury is told to assume the destroyed records would have been unfavorable to you), and even default judgments. This has turned winnable cases into losses.

Paper vs. Digital: The Retention Reality

Paper records degrade. They get lost in office moves. They get damaged by water, fire, or simple disorganization. A 5-year-old paper inspection form in a filing cabinet may be illegible, misfiled, or simply gone.

Digital records, properly backed up, last indefinitely. They're searchable, they don't degrade, and they can be produced in minutes during an audit or investigation. When OSHA asks for 3 years of monthly inspection records on Crane #47:

  • Paper company: Someone spends 2 days going through filing cabinets, finds most of them, discovers some are missing, and delivers a partial stack with an explanation
  • Digital company: Filter by equipment and date range, export as PDF, send. Five minutes.

The difference isn't just efficiency — it's credibility. Complete, organized, instantly-accessible records signal a professional operation. Incomplete paper records with gaps signal the opposite, even if you actually did every inspection.

The Simple Rule

When in doubt about how long to keep a crane record, the answer is: keep it. The cost of storing digital records is effectively zero. The cost of not having a record when you need it can be six or seven figures. Err on the side of keeping everything.

Records That Last Forever — Automatically

CraneCheck stores every inspection, maintenance record, and document digitally with automatic backups. No filing cabinets. No lost records. Audit-ready in seconds.
